item.title: Goodbye my love
item.description: My wife is now at peace. No more pain. After 1000+ lab results, 100+ operations and countless sleepless nights. And 7 years of fearing this Day. Now Im gonna look to the future and love my two daughters like there is no tomorrow. But there is, there is a tomorrow and its gonna be Ok.

item.title: I'm so sorry!
item.description: I have had a nasal polyp slowly decreasing my sense of smell for the last 3 years. I finally had it removed yesterday. OMG, all the smells. So many things have smells and I had forgotten. I shower everyday and always apply deodorant and clean clothes before work. I had no idea I was the smelly coworker until I work up this morning to the smell of hot garbage/ spoiled milk. After 10 minutes of searching I found it was coming from the lining of my Columbia jacket. I wear this to work everyday. I am so sorry!
item.dc:creator: TheGarbageManwich

item.title: They made a "Mistake"

item.title: Garlic, Bacon, and Caramelized Onion Mac & Cheese. GET you some.
item.description:
What happens when it’s date night and I have a fuckton of garlic confit and give zero shits about the raging cholesterol problem plaguing the nation? This. This is what happens. Garlic. Bacon. Onions. The best things come in threes. It’s the goddamn magic number, there’s a song about it and everything. I don’t make the rules, I just work here, so welcome aboard friends, and please allow me to guide you on this magical journey to a flavor carnival in your mouth. You’re welcome.
NOW, before we begin, a quick note about what follows. Everyone hates recipes blogs because of the 600 pages of bullshit stories about the author’s childhood on some dumb farm and their most recent pioneering fashion decisions. Luckily, I didn’t grow up on a farm, and my idea of fashion is my “going out” pair of sweatpants, so I’m not going to do the blog thing. What I WILL do is talk a little bit about food and how it works (at least in my experience). Read it, don’t read it, start a movement for or against it, I don’t care. You do you.
I will ALSO give you the recipe. Clearly. Look, there it is, right up there. A simple version (yep, that’s the boring white rectangle covered in my terrible handwriting) and a fuck you up fancy version with moving pictures and step by goddamn step instructions. Again, follow it as closely or as very not closely as you want. I’m not here to tell you what’s right or wrong. Just think of me as your personal kitchen Clippy popping up in the corner of the screen with passive aggressive suggestions. Yes this has lots of words to read, but imma try to make them words fun. Don’t like fun? Well I don’t like you. Despite being a chef, writing recipes is actually quite a challenge for me because when I cook for myself I generally don’t use them. Much like the rest of my life, I’m just fucking winging it. In fact, this is the first time I’ve made mac and cheese this way. All the ingredients SOUNDED good together, so I thought I'd see if they worked. So if you have questions/feedback/are curious about my thoughts on cilantro, let me know, and then I’ll let YOU know. We’re all in this together now. Lastly, I also think learning recipes is far less useful than learning techniques, because techniques can give you the freedom to go create your own recipes. Techniques teach you how, why, and when to do something, and once you understand those pieces, you’re golden. So, if I ever post about how to make any kind of food, there will be a “learning corner” highlighting a specific technique that I hope you’ll find valuable. Moving the fuck on.
The key ingredients - Garlic I’m using confit garlic I made two days ago in addition to fresh garlic, but you can of course use fresh garlic solo or roasted garlic as well. We are proudly a garlic safe space. Keep in mind fresh garlic will be stronger than confit or roasted. - Bacon. Or pancetta or guanciale, bacon’s loud and argumentative Italian cousins. I’m using 5 strips of bacon, though I HIGHLY recommend guanciale if you can find it. I sadly and obviously could not. - Onions Or shallots. Personally I'm using a yellow onion, as I find them to be ideal for caramelizing. - Cheese. Pick your favorites. Unless is bleu cheese. Don’t be an asshole. I’m using shredded mozzarella, fresh mozzarella, a little bit of mascarpone, and parmesan. - Pasta. How much pasta? I don’t know, how many damn people are you feeding? Do their friends want some? Or are you a Fatty McFat Fat that eats as much as I do and refuses to share? Do some pasta math. For tonight, I’ve got a pasta called strozzapreti, and used maybe two to three cups. (Yes, I know there's a bag of orzo in my ingredients photo, but I found the other pasta in my pantry and decided I wanted that one instead. DEAL with it.) - AP flour, half and half, chicken stock, white wine, panko bread crumbs, parsley, chives, - Salt. This shouldn’t need to be listed as an ingredient, almost everything you make should have at lease SOME salt. Seriously. - White sugar* - White wine vinegar* *more on the last two ingredients later The fully optional bonus ingredients - Mushrooms - Peas - Chiles - SHRIMPS, especially grilled ones for texture. - Lobster, if you’re filthy rich and think you’re too good for shrimps. - Chives/Basil/Whatever combination of fresh herbs you prefer - Lemon zest - Truffle, because we’re just setting money on fire now - Whatever else you crazy kids decide you want
Also - do you hate doing dishes? Of course you do, because it’s the fucking worst. This recipe requires just TWO pots (one needs to be oven safe), and a baking sheet. Yes you’ll have to clean a few other tools and maybe a bowl as well, but if you blow up your whole kitchen then that’s really a you problem at this point. And now, ONWARD! TO GLORY!
Mise en place, or “get your SHIT together”, in French. The biggest reason you destroy your kitchen and your food when you cook is because your shit AIN’T TOGETHER. Have a plan, have a system, get organized. Make a list, tape it to a cabinet. You think I’m fucking with you?
Nope. Why tape? Because gravity is a thing. But also this way I don’t ever lose my list, it’s out of the way, I don’t need to use my hands to see it, and unless I literally spray food ALL over the goddamn kitchen, it stays pretty clean.
Get EVERYTHING you’re going to use - equipment, ingredients, motivational quotes, dishes - out BEFORE you do ANYTHING. And THEN get your prep done. Have shit to cut up? Do it. Does anything need to be measured out or weighed? DO. IT. And now we're ready!
Stick your bacon on a foil-lined baking shit and cook it in an over at 400F until it's all crispy like. Strain and reserve the bacon fat (always save bacon fat, this isn’t amateur hour), eat half, and cut whatever's left into bits that will fit in your mouth. And nope, I'm not going to film myself putting a tray in an oven because if you can't figure THAT out, this post is very much NOT for you. Neither is operating heavy or even very light machinery. Pot + Fat + Onions + Salt + Time = Deliciousness. Fuck everything you learned in school and just that equation instead. That, and mitochondria is the power house of the cell. Caramelization is a slow process, so slice up them onions, put some fat (in my case confit garlic oil) in a pot, and let these guys get a nice bronze going. Salt is useful here because a. salt should always been in your food and b. it helps the onions release all their liquids so the sugars can start browning faster.
As your onions begin to caramelize, you'll see the build up of those sugars on the side and bottom of the pot. I am LITERALLY pointing at them. Once you see those, it's time to deglaze with a liquid. ANY liquid will work, but I'm using chicken stock and white wine because they taste better than stupid water. I’m using a Sauvignon Blanc, but any dry white wine is welcome at this party. I can’t drink because it gives me migraines in my face that makes me see squiggly rainbows, but if I could I would recommend pairing a DRY riesling with this meal, and almost anything else. They’re delicious. God I miss drugs. I mean wine. These sugars can burn VERY quickly, so I recommend a low flame. I moved my pot over so you could see what I'm working with.
Repeat this process until your onions look like those little caramels from the 1930's my grandmother used to keep in a huge bowl on her coffee table. I used to ask her why she didn't ever get new candy, and she used to tell me if your ass was square you'd shit bricks. Still haven't figured that one out If you get a big build up of caramelized onion bits on the side of your pan like I did, TILT it and let your liquid steam clean that deliciousness. See, gravity, still a thing. You can take your onions quite a bit darker than I chose to take mine if you prefer. Taste them and see what you like. Once you're happy, scrape those onions into a bowl and just let them chill for a minute. They're having a busy day. DON'T clean your damn pot, because we want to get as much of that leftover fond into our sauce as possible. Just keep your heat on low and follow me.
Next we sweat our veggies, and then make a roux, which is just combining equal parts fat and flour. Oh wait, what did we JUST get from cooking that bacon? Ya, fat, it’s not a trick question. As a general rule, 1 part roux thickens 8 parts liquid. So if you have one cup of roux, you’ll need 8 cups of liquid max to add to it. In this case that liquid is milk, and before you ask, FUCK no am I making 8 cups of it, who the hell am I feeding? All of Ohio? I’ve never even BEEN to Ohio. For tonight I’m using 2 TBS fat, 2 TBS flour, and a bit over 2 cups of liquid because pasta is starchy and will soak up a ton of moisture. Now that we understand fat + flour math, we first want to sweat some garlic in a teensy bit of our leftover bacon fat that’s gonna go in our roux. I’m not here to tell you much garlic to use, or not use. Go on your own garlic adventure. Bring a flute. Wear tights. Prance. There’s already salt in the bacon fat, so I’m not going to add any more at this stage, but we will still season*** as we go. Cook that garlic for just a minute or two. Add the rest of your bacon fat and all of your flour, and whisk that all up so it’s not lumpy. YOU can be lumpy. Your roux cannot. Cook the roux for maybe 3 minutes over medium-low heat just to knock a bit of the raw flour taste out of it, whisking frequently. Your mom may be easy, but trust me, burning a roux is easier. Think I’m being overly dramatic? Go ahead, crank the burner. Fly little bird, fly too close to the sun.
***A note about seasoning - when chefs talk about food being “seasoned”, they’re not referring to a delightful medley of spices and herbs. They’re talking about SALT. S-a-l-t salt. Learn it, know it, live it. And think about salt in layers. What are you putting in your dish? Are you adding something super salty, like soy sauce or, hmm, I dunno, BACON, which you have to consider? If you are, maybe under-season the other components a little bit to compensate. Maybe just cook a goddamn salt lick and I’ll go fuck myself. You have options.
Time to slowly lowly whisk your liquids. Liquids are added slowly because it avoids lumps, and actually makes a thicker sauce. Which is what we now have: a (slightly modified) bechamel, one of the five mother sauces. (Technically it’s half velouté have bechamel, but I won’t tell if you don’t). I am using around a cup each of chicken stock and half & half. This is the thickness I’m looking for. I know it seems a little watery, but remember we’re gonna add buckets of cheese to this, which will make for one thicccc saucy lady. ***A note about stocks and why you should make your own instead of buying them - I get it, chicken stock not only takes forever but it’s also boring. It’s the ugly marriage of time + dull. Ordinarily I am ALL about finding ways to take shortcuts in the kitchen, but this is not one of those moments. I seriously recommend you make your own stocks at home. It will take your cooking from “meh” to “SHOVE ALL THE FOOD IN MY FACE HOLE”. Songs and poems will be written about you. Suitors will begin to show up outside your door offering gifts just for the privilege of diddling your bits. Trust me, it’s the only reason I’m married. I’m happy to show you how I make these mythical stocks if you want. Just ask, and the fairy foodmother will make all your wishes come true. And don’t get me started on the fancy shmancy $15/jar bone broth bullshit things. Those can bibbity bobbity back right the fuck off out of here.
Now it’s time to let that magic happen. Get your cheese and put it ALL up in that sauce. I’m using mozzarella as my workhorse cheese today, the low moisture shredded version (I’m going to put some fresh slices on top before I stick it in the oven, but more on that later, calm your tits and stop getting ahead of me). I forgot to measure my cheese, but this bag claims to contain 2 cups and I used about half of it. So get out your calculators and figure that puppy out. Mix that shit up until it’s all melted, and then…fucking TASTE YOUR GODDAMN FOOD.
Always, always, ALWAYS taste your food AS YOU GO. Why? BECAUSE YOU STILL HAVE TIME TO CHANGE IT IF IT DOESN’T TASTE GOOD. Does it need salt? Acid? Bitterness? Sweetness? Umami, even? If it does, fucking ADD IT. If it doesn’t, keep fucking crushing it. You win at life, and I respect you.
***A note about flavors and how to balance them - there are five basic flavors that I’ve just listen above, and the way you balance them in cooking with this chart: So, if something is too salty, you balance that with bitterness, and vice versa, too bitter, you balance with salt. Same goes for acid/sour and sweetness. Umami is the intersection of all four, and it’s the holy grail of cooking, at least in my opinion. Parmesan cheese is a great example of a food high in umami, and fuck yes I am putting it in this dish. This is where the sugar and vinegar mentioned in the ingredients circle back in. Try out a little of each and see what it does to the taste. Emphasis on a LITTLE at a time. After you’ve seasoned (again, just talking about salt) add some other fun flavors if you want. Cayenne for heat, or black pepper. Nutmeg, for the adventurous. Make it yours, and make it tasty. CONGRATULATIONS, your sauce is done. Push that pot off to the backburner and leave it off the heat. Mix it occasionally if it looks lonely.
Next item on the agenda is pasta. Get it over high heat and SALT your pasta water, but don’t put oil in it, please. Why? Because what FLOATS in water?? Witches. And FUCKING OIL. It’s POINTLESS. Instead of staring at your own dumb reflection in the pasta water as it heats up, take this opportunity to clean whatever tools/equipment you’ve used so far. See? Clean as you go. Once your water is up to a boil, cook your pasta. I’m not going to tell you much more about how to do this because if you can’t pour pasta in hot water and wait, well, you’re probably the same person that has to watch flight attendants demonstrate how to use seatbelts before putting one on. Keep fighting the good fight, little buddy. When your pasta is a delightful al dente, strain it, and now I'm going to say something that's going to make several cultures hate me simultaneously..... Rinse your pasta.
Pasta has starch. Fucking fucktons of it. It's the magical stuff that makes sauce stick to it, and the reason why pasta water THICKENS those very same sauces. GUESS what this CHEESE SAUCE doesn't need any goddamn more of? Thickening. Or stickiness. In my experience, rinsing off pasta IN THIS CASE results in a creamier, less goopy, clumpy, congealed mac. So even though this would make my same Italian grandmother ACTUALLY shit bricks... Imma do it anyway.
While the pasta is cooking, get some panko in a bowl and add fat (oil, melted butter, duck fat, lard, pick your favorite) and mix that shit up. I'm using my hands, because I wash them CONSTANTLY, I'm cooking at home at not a restaurant, and I didn't feel like getting a spoon. Also fuck you, that's why.
Now begins our festival of delights! Put your rinsed pasta back in that warm pot and pour in some of that saucy sauce. Do it in increments and stir. Maybe you put all of it in, maybe only half, but please don’t just blindly pour it all in there like the robot overlords would want you to. Think, experiment, taste, and decide for yourself how much sauce is the right amount of sauce. Wake up sheeple. If you’re a boss and using mascarpone like I am, chuck a dollop in the pot and let your two cheeses get to know each other before the rest of the orgy arrives. TASTE YOUR FOOD, and adjust as necessary.
Mix in your fixins’. I’m using bacon, some of the smaller cloves of confit garlic, parsley, chives, and caramelized onions, obviously. And now, say it with me, can’t hear you in the back…taste your goddamn food. Please.
Now it’s time to make it raiiiiiiin sexy, sexy all over this damn thing. Fresh mozzarella can come too, if it wants. You want a pretty fine grate on the parm, I’m using a microplane for this, which I highly recommend getting because I use it constantly, mostly to accidentally zest my damn knuckles. And fresh nutmeg, Put your OVEN SAFE pot under the broiler and let that cheesy goodness get nice and bubbly. Should not take long at all, and keep an eye on it because the broiler is HOT and you’ve worked too damn hard to burn this shit now.
When your cheese is luxuriously melted and your breadcrumbs are golden, it’s time to pull your flavor carnival (carefully, for fuck’s sake) out of the oven and EAT YOU A SNACK. As far as garnish and plating goes, there are no rules here - let your conscience be your guide, make it nice, and please enjoy.
Learning Corner Highlighted Technique: Roux We’ve already talked a bit about making a roux today, but if you’re more of a whole-asser than a half-asser, here’s some MORE knowledge to pour in your head bucket.
Roux is, generally speaking, equal parts fat + flour, and is used as a base for many delicious things, from gumbo to thanksgiving gravy to mac and cheese, as we learned today. Roux comes in shades anywhere from “white” to “dark”, as pictured below. The longer you cook it, the darker it gets. 1 part roux will typically thicken 8 parts liquid, with a lighter roux having greater thickening power, and a darker roux having less. If you want to know the science behind why that happens, read this article and let Kenji enlighten you. He’s fucking amazing. https://www.seriouseats.com/a-brief-guide-to-roux To make a roux, all you have to do is combine fat and flour in a pan, stick it over heat, and whisk. Be attentive, because it burns like the goddamn dickens, and if you’re making a dark roux be prepared to hang out by the stove for a WHILE. Don’t think you need to know how to make a roux? Ever make a box of easy mac and put too much milk in the cheese food product powder crap and get noodles bathing in a pool of gross watery orange liquid? Whisk up a little melted butter with some flour in a pan and toss your neon noodles and milk in there and BOOM. Luxurious fake cheese sauce is yours, my friend.
I'll see you next time, boys and girls.
item.dc:creator: 20somethingelse
